title: MITRE ATT&CK and Automated Investigation: A Primer
meta_title: MITRE ATT&CK for the SOC | Nordic SOC
meta_description: How MITRE ATT&CK structures adversary behaviour into tactics and techniques, and how an AI SOC maps alerts to ATT&CK to speed up automated investigation.
excerpt: A practical primer on the MITRE ATT&CK framework for SOC teams: what tactics, techniques and procedures are, how ATT&CK mapping improves detection coverage, and how an AI SOC uses it to accelerate investigation.
author: Nordic SOC
hero_image_alt: Diagram of MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques mapped during automated SOC investigation

faq.a: MITRE ATT&CK is a globally accessible, free knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques maintained by MITRE, based on real-world observations. It organises attacker behaviour into tactics, techniques and sub-techniques across Enterprise, Mobile and ICS matrices.

faq.q: What is the difference between tactics, techniques and procedures?
faq.a: Tactics describe an attacker's goal at a given stage, such as Initial Access or Exfiltration. Techniques describe how that goal is achieved, often with sub-techniques. Procedures are the concrete, observed implementations of a technique by a specific adversary or tool.

faq.q: How does ATT&CK mapping improve threat detection?
faq.a: Mapping detections and alerts to ATT&CK exposes which techniques are covered and which are blind spots, adds intent and context to each alert, and connects related events into a single attack narrative rather than isolated signals.

faq.q: How does an AI SOC use MITRE ATT&CK?
faq.a: An AI SOC maps every alert to ATT&CK tactics and techniques automatically, correlates related telemetry, and produces an explainable, structured incident. This speeds up investigation and lets incidents be prioritised by likely severity and attack stage.

faq.q: Is MITRE ATT&CK free to use?
faq.a: Yes. MITRE ATT&CK is a free, openly maintained knowledge base, so any security team can adopt it as a common framework without licensing barriers.
